rationale: The citing paper uses Hadar, Sood, and Fox (2013) as its closest prior study, not just as background. It explicitly summarizes Hadar et al.'s core result (subjective knowledge predicts investment preference controlling objective knowledge) and then identifies a gap: that prior work did not measure risk perceptions. The new paper builds on that gap by modeling risk-perception components and linking understanding to portfolio allocations, including conditions where greater understanding does not always increase preference. This is a meaningful conceptual continuation of the cited work.
relation_description: The citing paper extends Hadar et al. (2013) by moving from subjective knowledge effects on investment preference to a risk-perception account of why those effects occur. It adds new risk measures and portfolio-choice evidence to show when perceived understanding changes expected risk and can reverse preference patterns depending on risk tolerance.
